attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of increasing pain in the left side of the scrotum and some urinary frequency. He also mentions symptoms of poor stream and prolonged micturition for the last few months. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 120/65 mmHg, and temperature 38.2°C. His abdomen is soft to palpation, but there is some tenderness behind the left testis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in his assessment?
options: CT of kidneys, ureters and bladder
attributes.explanation:
In this case, the most appropriate next step is a digital rectal examination. The patient presents with symptoms suggestive of epididymitis (scrotal pain and fever) along with lower urinary tract symptoms (poor stream, prolonged micturition) that have been present for months. In older men, epididymitis is often associated with underlying urinary tract pathology, particularly benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). A digital rectal examination is essential to assess the size, consistency, and tenderness of the prostate, which could provide valuable information about potential obstruction or infection. This would be done as part of the physical examination, before any investigations are considered.

Urine microscopy and culture are important investigations in suspected epididymitis and would likely be performed, but they should follow a thorough clinical examination first, including digital rectal examination. These tests can help identify the causative organism and guide antibiotic therapy.

Ultrasound of groin and scrotum can be useful in differentiating epididymitis from testicular torsion and other scrotal pathologies. However, given the patient's age and the presence of lower urinary tract symptoms, assessing the prostate should take priority. Torsion would be a very rare event in a 65-year-old man.

CT of kidneys, ureters and bladder is not typically the first-line investigation for epididymitis or lower urinary tract symptoms. It may be considered if there is suspicion of upper urinary tract involvement or complications, but this is not suggested by the current presentation.

Urinary nucleic acid amplification tests are useful for detecting sexually transmitted infections like chlamydia and gonorrhea, which are common causes of epididymitis in younger men. However, in an older man with lower urinary tract symptoms, these are less likely to be the cause, and a digital rectal examination would be more informative as the next step.
learningPoints: Digital rectal examination is an essential part of the assessment in men with urinary symptoms and suspected prostate pathology.
Epididymitis in older men is often associated with underlying urinary tract pathology, particularly benign prostatic hyperplasia.
Urine microscopy and culture are important investigations in suspected epididymitis, but should follow a thorough clinical examination.
references: Rupp TJ, Leslie SW. Epididymitis. In: StatPearls [Online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430814/
Walker NA, Challacombe B. Managing epididymo-orchitis in general practice. Practitioner. 2013 Apr;257(1760):21-5, 2-3. PMID: 23724748. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23724748/
